content.problemStatement:
Given an array AAA of size NNN.

You can do the following operation on the array:


Pick any 222 **distinct** indices iii and jjj such that Ai=AjA_i=A_jAi​=Aj​;
Change all the elements between the indices iii and jjj to AiA_iAi​, i.e, for all kkk (i≤k≤j)(i \leq k \leq j)(i≤k≤j), set Ak=AiA_k=A_iAk​=Ai​.
Find the **minimum** number of operations required to **sort** the array in non-decreasing order. If it is not possible to sort the array, print −1-1−1 instead.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN - the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, the **minimum** number of operations required to **sort** the array. If it is not possible to sort the array, print −1-1−1 instead.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111: We can sort the array using 222 operations.

In the first operation select indices 111 and 333. After this operation, the array will be A=[1,1,1,3,4,3]A = [1,1,1,3,4,3]A=[1,1,1,3,4,3].
In the second operation select indices 444 and 666. After this operation, the array will be A=[1,1,1,3,3,3]A = [1,1,1,3,3,3]A=[1,1,1,3,3,3].

Thus, the array is sorted using 222 operations. It can be proven that the array cannot be sorted in less than 222 operations.
Test Case 222: It is not possible to sort the array using any number of operations.
